note_data.title: Chemical bonding
keyPoints: Compound - 2 or more elements chemically joined together - Contains Covalent or Ionic Bonds
Mixtures - 2 or more substances which are not chemically joined together
Pure Substances have: Fixed composition, Fixed Mp/Bp, Produces only 1 spot on chromatogram, All molecules same (equal)
Mixture has: Variable Composition, Variable Bp/Mp, 2 or more spots on Chromatogram, 2 or more diff. molecules
Solid & Liquid solution → Crystallization (Obtaining solid) – Solute
Solid & Liquid solution → Distillation (Obtaining Liquid) – Solvent
Mixture of Organic Compounds → Chromatography (Eg. Colour Dyes)
Filtration possible because: Liquid particles small enough to go thru filter paper pores, Solid particles too large

definitions.definition: 2 or more elements chemically joined together - Contains Covalent or Ionic Bonds
2 or more substances which are not chemically joined together
no more solid can dissolve in it

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give details about the structure or bonding without explanation

modelAnswer.question: Explain why sodium chloride has a high melting point but does not conduct electricity when solid. [3 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Sodium chloride has a giant ionic structure with strong electrostatic forces between oppositely charged ions, requiring a lot of energy to break. When solid, the ions are in fixed positions and cannot move, so it cannot conduct electricity.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Giant ionic structure/strong electrostatic forces
Mark 2: A lot of energy needed to break forces
Mark 3: Ions in fixed positions/cannot move when solid

commonMistakes: Confusing compounds (chemically joined) with mixtures (not chemically joined)
Not understanding that pure substances have fixed Mp/Bp while mixtures have variable Bp/Mp
Mixing up when to use crystallization (obtaining solid) versus distillation (obtaining liquid)
examinerTips: Any question that wants the method to obtain (make) crystals, the process will be 'Crystalisation'
The constant temperature of distillation process = Boiling Point of pure liquid
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Dot-and-cross diagrams showing electron arrangements in ionic compounds (e.g., NaCl, MgO) and covalent molecules (e.g., H2O, CH4). Also include simple diagrams of giant ionic, metallic, and covalent structures.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain the difference in melting points between sodium chloride and magnesium oxide using bonding theory
N/D 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question on identifying the type of bonding in ammonia molecules
M/J 2022 P2 Q3(c) (4 marks) — students had to draw dot-and-cross diagrams for water and predict its bond angle
N/D 2023 P2 Q2 (5 marks) — question required explanation of electrical conductivity in metals versus ionic compounds in different states

workedExample.question: Draw the dot-and-cross diagram for ammonia (NH₃) and explain why it has a pyramidal shape. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Count total valence electrons - nitrogen has 5, each hydrogen has 1, so total = 5 + (3×1) = 8 electrons
Step 2: Draw nitrogen in center with 3 hydrogens around it. Show nitrogen's electrons as dots, hydrogen's as crosses
Step 3: Place 3 bonding pairs between N and each H, plus 1 lone pair on nitrogen
Step 4: Explain shape - 4 electron pairs around nitrogen (3 bonding + 1 lone pair) arrange tetrahedrally, but lone pair repels more strongly, creating pyramidal molecular shape
workedExample.answer: Correct dot-and-cross diagram showing N with 3 bonding pairs to H atoms and 1 lone pair. Shape is pyramidal because the lone pair of electrons repels the bonding pairs more strongly than bonding pairs repel each other, pushing the hydrogen atoms closer together.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct electron count, 1 mark for correct diagram showing lone pair, 1 mark for stating pyramidal shape, 1 mark for explaining lone pair repulsion

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused intermolecular forces with intramolecular bonding' — always distinguish between bonds within molecules and forces between molecules when explaining physical properties

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often drew incorrect dot-and-cross diagrams by not showing the origin of electrons clearly' — always use different symbols (dots/crosses) to show which atom donated each electron in the bonding pair

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates frequently stated that ionic compounds conduct electricity without specifying the state' — always mention that ionic compounds only conduct when molten or dissolved, not as solids
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must master dot-and-cross diagrams showing: (1) All valence electrons as dots for one element, crosses for another (2) Shared electron pairs in covalent bonds positioned between atoms (3) Lone pairs clearly positioned on relevant atoms (4) Correct total electron count. For ionic compounds, show complete electron transfer with resulting charges. Include examples like H₂O, NH₃, CH₄ for covalent and NaCl, MgO for ionic.
note_data.quickRecap: Compounds are elements chemically joined together while mixtures are substances not chemically joined. Pure substances have fixed properties while mixtures have variable properties. Different separation techniques are used: filtration for insoluble solids, crystallization to obtain solids, distillation to obtain liquids, and fractional distillation for miscible liquids.
